---
title: "19 · ε-Greedy: Exploration without Exploring Starts"
subtitle: "A small constant ε of random action guarantees coverage without artificial start states."
lesson-id: "mfrl.epsilon-greedy"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.mc-exploring-starts"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state the MC ES algorithm from Lesson 18?
2. Do you understand why deterministic policies create the *exploration
   problem* — some $(s, a)$ pairs are never visited?
3. Are you comfortable with mixed (stochastic) policies $\pi(a \mid s)$?

If shaky, revisit Lessons 17, 18, and 3 (policies).
:::

## Touch

The exploring-starts trick said: "let me teleport the agent anywhere."
That's unrealistic. The $\varepsilon$-greedy trick says: "let the agent
*usually* be greedy, but occasionally pick a random action." This adds
exploration *during* the trajectory, not at its start.

Slide $\varepsilon$ from 0 to 1. Watch the agent's behaviour shift from
"perfectly greedy" to "essentially random."

```{ojs}
viewof epsilon = Inputs.range([0, 1], {value: 0.2, step: 0.01, label: "ε"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof seedBtn = Inputs.button("Run new episode")
```

```{ojs}
{
  seedBtn;
  const N = 4;
  const goal = [3, 3];
  const next = (i, j, a) => {
    if (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) return [i, j];
    let ni = i, nj = j;
    if (a === 0 && i > 0) ni = i - 1;
    if (a === 1 && i < N - 1) ni = i + 1;
    if (a === 2 && j > 0) nj = j - 1;
    if (a === 3 && j < N - 1) nj = j + 1;
    return [ni, nj];
  };
  // "Greedy" policy: always head toward goal (down or right depending on position)
  const greedy = (i, j) => {
    if (i < goal[0]) return 1; // down
    if (j < goal[1]) return 3; // right
    return 1;
  };
  let i = 0, j = 0;
  const path = [[i, j]];
  for (let t = 0; t < 25; t++) {
    if (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) break;
    let a;
    if (Math.random() < epsilon) {
      a = Math.floor(Math.random() * 4);
    } else {
      a = greedy(i, j);
    }
    const [ni, nj] = next(i, j, a);
    i = ni; j = nj;
    path.push([i, j]);
  }
  // Render grid
  const cell = 50;
  const cells = [];
  for (let r = 0; r < N; r++) for (let c = 0; c < N; c++) {
    const isGoal = (r === goal[0] && c === goal[1]);
    const onPath = path.some(([pi, pj]) => pi === r && pj === c);
    const visits = path.filter(([pi, pj]) => pi === r && pj === c).length;
    const bg = isGoal ? "#ffd966" : (onPath ? `rgba(135, 206, 250, ${Math.min(1, visits / 5)})` : "white");
    cells.push(html`<div style="width:${cell}px; height:${cell}px; background:${bg}; border:1px solid #999; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center; font-family:monospace;">
      ${isGoal ? "★" : (visits > 0 ? visits : "")}
    </div>`);
  }
  return html`<div>
    <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(${N}, ${cell}px); gap:0; width:max-content; margin:1rem auto;">
      ${cells}
    </div>
    <div style="text-align:center; font-size:0.9rem; color:#666;">ε = ${epsilon}, episode length ${path.length} steps. Cell number = visits this episode.</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Try $\varepsilon = 0$ (perfectly greedy — direct path to goal). Try
$\varepsilon = 1$ (random walk; might never reach goal). Try
$\varepsilon = 0.2$ (mostly greedy, with occasional detours).

## Wonder

1. With $\varepsilon = 0$ the agent always picks the greedy action.
   What's the problem with that, given that we *want* an optimal policy?
2. With $\varepsilon = 1$ the agent never exploits any knowledge.
   What's the problem with that?
3. Is there a "right" $\varepsilon$? Should it depend on the MDP, on the
   stage of training, or both?

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Defining $\varepsilon$-greedy

Given a deterministic greedy action $a^* = \arg\max_a q(s, a)$ and
exploration parameter $\varepsilon \in [0, 1]$, write the
$\varepsilon$-greedy policy as a probability distribution over actions.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
With probability $1 - \varepsilon$ act greedily; with probability
$\varepsilon$ act uniformly random over all $|A|$ actions.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$\pi_\varepsilon(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} 1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{|A|} & \text{if } a = a^* \\ \dfrac{\varepsilon}{|A|} & \text{if } a \ne a^* \end{cases}$$

(The "+ $\varepsilon/|A|$" term in the first case accounts for the chance
that the *random* action coincidentally equals the greedy.) Verify
$\sum_a \pi_\varepsilon(a \mid s) = 1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon/|A| + (|A| - 1)\varepsilon/|A| = 1$. ✓
</details>

### Problem 2 — Coverage under $\varepsilon$-greedy

Show that for any $\varepsilon > 0$, every action has *positive*
probability at every state under $\pi_\varepsilon$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Look at the formula in Problem 1.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Every action has probability *at least* $\varepsilon/|A| > 0$ (the
non-greedy ones have *exactly* this; the greedy has more). So if every
state $s$ is reached infinitely often, every $(s, a)$ pair is visited
infinitely often. **No exploring starts needed** — the random action
choices provide coverage.

This is the entire point of $\varepsilon$-greedy: trade *initialisation*
flexibility for *runtime* randomness.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Exploration-exploitation tradeoff

Why is small $\varepsilon$ "more exploitation, less exploration" and
large $\varepsilon$ the opposite? Sketch what happens to total reward at
each extreme.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\varepsilon = 0$: pure exploit. $\varepsilon = 1$: pure explore.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- **$\varepsilon \to 0$ (exploit).** Agent always picks current best;
  but if "current best" is wrong (because $\hat q$ hasn't seen better
  options), agent never discovers them. Locked into local optimum.
- **$\varepsilon \to 1$ (explore).** Agent ignores its knowledge entirely;
  total reward is bad even if $\hat q$ is correct.
- **Intermediate.** Best total reward in practice. Exact optimum depends
  on MDP and training horizon.

This is the **[exploration-exploitation tradeoff](/glossary.qmd#exploration-exploitation)** —
*the* central tension of RL. Almost every algorithmic trick later in
this track is a reframing of it.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Decay schedules

Why might you want $\varepsilon$ to *decrease over time*? What does the
schedule $\varepsilon_t = 1/t$ accomplish?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Early in training, $\hat q$ is bad, you want to explore. Late in
training, $\hat q$ is good, you want to exploit.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Early:** $\varepsilon$ near 1 → agent explores broadly, gathers data
about all $(s, a)$ pairs.

**Late:** $\varepsilon$ near 0 → agent exploits its (now good) $\hat q$;
total reward approaches optimal.

**Schedule $\varepsilon_t = 1/t$:** $\sum_t \varepsilon_t = \infty$
(infinite exploration in expectation) but $\varepsilon_t \to 0$
(asymptotic greediness). This is the **GLIE** condition (Greedy in the
Limit with Infinite Exploration), sufficient for MC ε-greedy to converge
to $\pi^*$.

Other schedules: $\varepsilon_t = c / \log t$ (slower decay), or
piecewise (e.g.\ $\varepsilon = 0.1$ for first $10^6$ steps, then
$0.01$). Real systems are tuned empirically.
</details>

### Problem 5 — MC ε-greedy algorithm

Write the algorithm in pseudocode in 8 lines or fewer. (No exploring
starts.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
```
initialize q̂(s, a) = 0 for all (s, a)
initialize ε ∈ (0, 1) (and optionally a decay schedule)
for episode = 1, 2, ...:
   π_ε(a | s) ← ε/|A| + (1 - ε) · 1[a = argmax_a' q̂(s, a')]
   generate trajectory (S_0, A_0, R_0, ..., S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_{T-1}) under π_ε from a fixed start
   compute G_t backward as in Lesson 18 Problem 3
   for each visit to (s, a) in the trajectory:    # every-visit
      update q̂(s, a) by adding G_t to running average
   optionally decay ε
return greedy policy w.r.t. q̂
```
**No model used. No exploring starts. Just $\varepsilon$ random actions
plus every-visit averaging.** This is the workhorse algorithm at the
heart of every modern model-free RL method.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
$\varepsilon$-greedy is the simplest possible answer to "how do I
explore without teleporting?" Every more sophisticated exploration
strategy — Boltzmann/softmax, UCB, Thompson sampling, intrinsic
motivation — is a refinement of the same idea: occasional non-greedy
action choice.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**[ε-greedy policy](/glossary.qmd#epsilon-greedy).** For exploration
parameter $\varepsilon \in [0, 1]$ and current action-value estimate
$\hat q$:
$$\pi_\varepsilon(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} 1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon/|A| & a = \arg\max_{a'} \hat q(s, a') \\ \varepsilon/|A| & \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}$$

**MC ε-greedy.** Same as MC ES but the *behaviour policy* is
$\pi_\varepsilon$ rather than the deterministic greedy. No exploring
starts assumed: the agent starts at any fixed initial state and explores
through the random action choices.

**GLIE convergence.** With a schedule
$\varepsilon_t \to 0$ such that $\sum_t \varepsilon_t = \infty$ (e.g.\
$\varepsilon_t = 1/t$), MC ε-greedy converges almost surely to $\pi^*$.

:::

A verse:

> *Mostly greedy, sometimes random;*
> *Coverage from the dice, not from teleport.*
> *As ε decays, exploit takes over —*
> *Greedy in the limit, exploring forever.*

## Apply

### Worked example — MC ε-greedy on the 4-state corridor

States $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, $\gamma = 0.5$, reward 1 at $s_3$
(absorbing). Initial $\hat q = 0$ everywhere. $\varepsilon = 0.5$ (high
to start). Always start at $s_0$.

**Episode 1.** $\hat q$ all zero, so $\arg\max$ is arbitrary; suppose it
picks $L$. With $\varepsilon = 0.5$ each step is 50% greedy ($L$) and 50%
random.

Possible trajectory: $s_0 \xrightarrow{R} s_1 \xrightarrow{L} s_0 \xrightarrow{R} s_1 \xrightarrow{R} s_2 \xrightarrow{R} s_3$
(reward 1, absorbed). Returns:
$G_4 = 1, G_3 = 0.5, G_2 = 0.25, G_1 = 0.125, G_0 = 0.0625$.

Update $\hat q$ at each visit. After this one episode, $\hat q(s_2, R) = 1$,
$\hat q(s_1, R) = 0.5$, $\hat q(s_0, R) = 0.0625 + 0.125 = $ wait, let's
re-verify: visit times for $(s_0, R)$ are $t = 0$ and $t = 2$ (returns
$G_0 = 0.0625, G_2 = 0.25$). Average = $(0.0625 + 0.25)/2 = 0.156$. Etc.

Greedy actions are now mostly $R$. Behaviour policy still has 50% random.

**Episode 10.** With more episodes, $\hat q$ stabilises:
$\hat q(s_0, R) \approx 0.125, \hat q(s_1, R) \approx 0.25, \hat q(s_2, R) \approx 1.0$.
$\hat q(s_*, L)$ stays near 0 because $L$ trajectories rarely reach goal.

Greedy = $R$ everywhere. With $\varepsilon$ decay (e.g.\ to 0.05 by
episode 100), behaviour converges to greedy.

**Episode 100.** $\hat q$ near $q^*$; greedy policy is optimal;
$\varepsilon = 0.05$ means 95% optimal behaviour. Mission complete
without ever teleporting.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute $\pi_\varepsilon(a^* \mid s)$ and
$\pi_\varepsilon(a' \mid s)$ for $\varepsilon = 0.2$, $|A| = 4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi_\varepsilon(a^* \mid s) = 0.8 + 0.2/4 = 0.85$.
$\pi_\varepsilon(a' \mid s) = 0.2/4 = 0.05$ for each non-greedy.
Check: $0.85 + 3 \cdot 0.05 = 1.0$. ✓
</details>

**W2.** With $\varepsilon = 0.1$ and 4 actions, what's the probability
that the agent is *not* greedy on a given step?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Probability of non-greedy action = $\varepsilon \cdot (|A| - 1)/|A| = 0.1 \cdot 3/4 = 0.075$.
**About 7.5% of steps are non-greedy** — even though "ε" is 10%, because
the random pick sometimes coincidentally equals the greedy.
</details>

**W3.** Why doesn't $\varepsilon$-greedy converge to $\pi^*$ if
$\varepsilon$ is held *fixed* at, say, 0.1?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Behaviour policy is always 10% random — even at the limit. The MC ε-greedy
algorithm finds the *best $\varepsilon$-greedy policy*, not the
*unconstrained optimal*. With fixed $\varepsilon > 0$, the asymptotic
$\pi_{\hat q}$ is greedy w.r.t.\ $q^{\pi_\varepsilon}$ — different from
$q^*$ in general (because suboptimal actions still get chosen 10% of the
time, dragging values down). To converge to $\pi^*$, you need
$\varepsilon \to 0$ (GLIE).
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Suppose $\hat q(s, a^*) - \hat q(s, a') = \Delta$ for all
non-greedy $a'$ (uniform action gap). What's the *expected immediate
loss* per step under $\pi_\varepsilon$ vs. greedy?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each non-greedy action loses $\Delta$ in expectation. Probability of
non-greedy = $\varepsilon (|A| - 1)/|A|$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Expected loss per step $= \varepsilon \cdot \frac{|A| - 1}{|A|} \cdot \Delta$.
For $\varepsilon = 0.1, |A| = 4, \Delta = 1$: loss $= 0.075$ per step.

In a 100-step episode, total loss $\approx 7.5$. **This is why
$\varepsilon$-decay matters in production**: holding $\varepsilon$ at 0.1
forever wastes 7.5% of total reward, even after $\hat q$ has converged.
</details>

**P2.** *Show that $\pi_\varepsilon$-improvement is monotone for any
fixed $\varepsilon$.* Specifically: if $\pi$ is an $\varepsilon$-greedy
policy w.r.t.\ some $\hat q$, and $\pi'$ is the $\varepsilon$-greedy policy
w.r.t.\ $q_\pi$, then $v_{\pi'} \ge v_\pi$ pointwise.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Same proof as Lesson 14's policy improvement theorem, but $\pi'$ is now
a stochastic mixture rather than greedy.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$T^{\pi'} v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi'(a | s) [r(s, a) + \gamma \sum P(s' | s, a) v_\pi(s')]$.
$\pi'$ assigns weight $1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon/|A|$ to the greedy
action and $\varepsilon/|A|$ to the rest. Compute:
$$T^{\pi'} v_\pi(s) = (1 - \varepsilon) \max_a [\ldots] + \frac{\varepsilon}{|A|}\sum_a [\ldots]$$
The second term equals the policy-evaluation step *under any uniform
policy*, which equals... (skip details). Sutton & Barto §5.4 show that
$T^{\pi'} v_\pi \ge T^\pi v_\pi = v_\pi$, hence $v_{\pi'} \ge v_\pi$.
**ε-greedy improvement is also monotone**, but the limit is the
*ε-greedy optimal* policy, not the unconstrained optimum.
</details>

**P3.** Compare convergence speed of MC Basic, MC ES, and MC ε-greedy
on a 4-state corridor. (Sketch only.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- **MC Basic** ($N$ trajectories per $(s, a)$, exploring starts). Each
  outer iteration: $|S| \cdot |A| \cdot N = 8N$ trajectories. Many
  outer iterations.
- **MC ES** (every-visit + exploring starts). Each trajectory used many
  times. Reduce trajectories per outer iteration by ~3×.
- **MC ε-greedy.** No teleport. Single trajectory per "outer step,"
  many outer steps. Total environment interactions comparable to MC ES,
  but **realistic** (no teleport).

In wall-clock terms, MC ε-greedy is the only one usable in real
applications. MC ES is a teaching device; MC Basic is its convergence-proof
backbone.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *Boltzmann (softmax) exploration.* Replace the
$\varepsilon$-greedy choice with $\pi_\tau(a | s) \propto \exp(\hat q(s, a)/\tau)$
for temperature $\tau > 0$. Compare its exploration behaviour to
$\varepsilon$-greedy. When is each preferable?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Boltzmann.** Probability of action $a$ scales with its $\hat q$-value
(soft greedy). Better-than-random actions are favoured *proportionally*,
not just "the best one wins."
- $\tau \to 0$: deterministic greedy.
- $\tau \to \infty$: uniform random.

**vs. ε-greedy.** ε-greedy treats all non-greedy actions equally;
Boltzmann is "smart random" — explores good non-greedy actions more
than bad ones.

**When ε-greedy wins.** When $\hat q$ is poorly scaled (large
$\hat q$-differences swamp the temperature) or when you just want a
simple knob.

**When Boltzmann wins.** When the *ranking* of actions matters more than
the gap, especially in policy-gradient methods (REINFORCE, Lesson 36) —
which is where you'll meet the soft-max policy parameterisation.
</details>

**S2.** *UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) exploration.* Instead of random
exploration, pick the action that maximises
$\hat q(s, a) + c \sqrt{\log N(s) / N(s, a)}$, where $N(s)$ is visits to
$s$ and $N(s, a)$ visits to $(s, a)$. Sketch why this is "intelligent
exploration" — and what it costs.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The bonus $\sqrt{\log N(s)/N(s, a)}$ is a *confidence width*: it shrinks
fast as you visit $(s, a)$, so under-visited actions get explored even
when their $\hat q$ is mediocre. UCB has *deterministic* exploration —
no random choice needed.

**Cost.** Need to track visit counts; not always practical when state
space is huge or function approximation is used. Also, the regret bound
holds for *bandits* (single state); generalisations to MDPs are harder
(e.g.\ MBIE-EB, R-Max).

**Where it's used.** AlphaGo/AlphaZero's MCTS uses UCB-style action
selection in the tree search. UCB is the gold standard when you can
maintain counts.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
"$\varepsilon$-greedy" first appeared in Thompson's 1933 paper on
sequential clinical trials — decades before anything called "RL" existed.
The exploration-exploitation tradeoff was already understood as the
central problem in *experimental design*: you want to learn which
treatment works (explore) without giving sick patients the bad one
(exploit). RL inherited the framing.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$\varepsilon$-greedy converges to $\pi^*$ for any $\varepsilon$."**
   Only with $\varepsilon \to 0$ (GLIE). Fixed $\varepsilon$ converges
   to the *$\varepsilon$-greedy optimal*, not the unconstrained optimum.
2. **"Random action means random outcome."** No — random *action*; the
   *transition* is still governed by $P$. Same MDP, just a different
   way to act.
3. **"$\varepsilon$-greedy and exploring starts are equivalent."** Both
   provide exploration, but $\varepsilon$-greedy is realistic and
   exploring starts requires teleportation.
4. **"You should use a fixed $\varepsilon = 0.1$."** Often a starting
   default, but real systems decay — typically piecewise-linear or
   $1/t$.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **$\varepsilon$-greedy** = "act greedily with prob. $1 - \varepsilon$,
   uniformly at random otherwise."
2. **Coverage without teleportation.** Every $(s, a)$ has positive
   probability under $\pi_\varepsilon$.
3. **GLIE schedule** ($\varepsilon \to 0$, $\sum \varepsilon = \infty$)
   gives convergence to $\pi^*$.
4. **Exploration-exploitation tradeoff.** Small $\varepsilon$ exploits
   too much; large $\varepsilon$ explores too much. The right balance
   depends on the problem and training stage.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you write the $\pi_\varepsilon$ formula and the MC ε-greedy
   pseudocode without notes?
2. Did you compute the W1, P1 quantitative questions on your own?
3. Can you explain *why* fixed $\varepsilon$ does not converge to $\pi^*$?

- **3 yes:** move on to Lesson 20 (Robbins-Monro and stochastic
  approximation).
- **2 yes:** redo P2 — the monotone improvement argument is subtle and
  important.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* Problems 3 and 4 carefully.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **Lesson 18 (MC ES).** $\varepsilon$-greedy replaces the
  exploring-starts assumption; everything else is the same.
- **Lesson 8 (action values).** $\hat q$ drives the greedy choice.

### What this unlocks

- **Lesson 20-22 — Stochastic approximation.** Robbins-Monro generalises
  MC averaging to "find a fixed point from noisy samples"; foundational
  for TD methods.
- **Lessons 24, 26 — SARSA, Q-learning.** Both use $\varepsilon$-greedy
  for exploration; SARSA learns *on-policy* (uses $\pi_\varepsilon$ as
  its target), Q-learning learns *off-policy* (uses greedy as target,
  $\pi_\varepsilon$ for behaviour).
- **All deep RL.** DQN uses $\varepsilon$-greedy with annealing.
  PPO/SAC use entropy bonuses (a "soft" cousin of $\varepsilon$).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** $\varepsilon$-greedy or its softmax cousin is the
exploration mechanism in *every* modern RL algorithm. DQN's epsilon
schedule is famous: linearly anneal from $1.0$ to $0.1$ over the first
1M frames, then hold. Variants (NoisyNets, parameter noise, intrinsic
motivation) refine this but the structure is unchanged.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning. The
exploration-exploitation tradeoff that this lesson formalises is the
central tension of the entire field.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §5.4. Cite freely; never transcribe.
- Sutton & Barto §2.2 (bandits intro to ε-greedy), §5.4 (MC control with
  ε-greedy).
- Touchstones: Sutton & Barto's pedagogy; Watkins's Q-learning paper
  (1989) for the $\varepsilon$-greedy + off-policy combination.
:::
